profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: The paper addresses safety assessment and risk management in industrial or transportation systems, focusing on developing methods to analyze and improve safety performance through human factors analysis and systematic evaluation approaches.
profiles.domain: Safety science, occupational safety, human factors
profiles.method_summary: The study employs survey questionnaires, interviews, and statistical analysis to develop and validate a safety assessment framework. The methodology includes data collection from industrial settings, development of safety evaluation criteria, and empirical validation of proposed methods.
datasets: Primary data collected through questionnaires and interviews from industrial settings (specific details not fully readable from OCR text)
tasks: Safety assessment, risk analysis, human factors evaluation
metrics: Safety performance indicators, risk assessment scores, statistical significance measures
baselines: Traditional safety assessment methods, existing risk analysis frameworks

main_claims.claim_text: The proposed safety assessment method provides more comprehensive evaluation compared to traditional approaches
Human factors significantly influence safety outcomes in industrial environments
The developed framework enables systematic identification and prioritization of safety risks
Statistical analysis confirms the validity of the proposed safety evaluation criteria

limitations.text: Sample size may be limited due to access constraints in industrial settings
Generalizability may be restricted to specific industrial sectors
Self-reported data from questionnaires may introduce response bias

future_work: Extension of the proposed framework to other industrial sectors
Longitudinal studies to assess long-term safety performance improvements
Integration of advanced technologies for real-time safety monitoring
Comparative studies across different organizational cultures
profiles.notes: Paper appears to be from Iranian safety science research community circa 2001. The OCR text contains Persian language content with technical safety terminology. The study develops a systematic approach to safety evaluation that integrates human factors considerations.

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Investigate pedestrian behaviors at various urban crosswalks and pedestrian perceptions toward different pedestrian facilities (signalized/unsignalized intersection crosswalks, midblock crosswalks, physical barriers, shelters, colored paving, warning signs) in a divided urban boulevard next to Michigan State University
profiles.domain: Transportation Engineering, Pedestrian Safety, Urban Planning
profiles.method_summary: Combined observational study using video cameras (8 cameras covering 1km corridor) to record pedestrian movements and user survey (711 respondents) to assess pedestrian perceptions. Data collected on Grand River Avenue (M-43), East Lansing, MI, including spatial and temporal crossing compliance rates calculated from video analysis and survey responses
method_family: Empirical observational study with survey research
datasets: Survey data from 711 pedestrians (daily and occasional users)
Video observation data from 8 video cameras
Grand River Avenue study site: 1km divided boulevard section from Abbott to Bogue Streets, East Lansing, Michigan
tasks: Gender and age factor analysis on responses
metrics: Spatial Crossing Compliance Rate (SCCR) - percentage of pedestrians using designated crosswalk locations
Temporal Crossing Compliance Rate (TCCR) - percentage complying with pedestrian signal indication
Survey response rates and preference percentages

main_claims.claim_text: Unsignalized midblock crosswalks showed the highest pedestrian preference (83% reported preference to cross) and high crossing compliance rate (71.2%)
Crosswalk location relative to pedestrian origin/destination is the most influential decision factor (90% of respondents indicated distance to destination as primary factor)
Proper traffic control (pedestrian signals) significantly influences pedestrian decisions to cross at specific locations (74% reported influence)
Physical barriers (vegetation and concrete barriers) influence the crossing decisions of a significant number of pedestrians (65%)
There is good agreement between survey responses and observed pedestrian behavior, validating the survey methodology

limitations.text: Study site was limited to one specific urban corridor (Grand River Avenue) which may not be representative of all urban environments
Survey response rate was 17.9% (897 completed from 5000 distributed), which may introduce non-response bias
Study population primarily consisted of university affiliates (MSU), limiting generalizability to other populations
Data collection excluded rainy days to protect equipment, potentially missing pedestrian behavior under wet conditions
User preferences may not correlate highly with safety considerations - relationship between safety and pedestrian acceptance needs further analysis

future_work: Conduct additional crash and conflict analysis to assess the value of pedestrian warning signs and provide warrants for their use
Examine relationship between safety and pedestrian acceptance in future research
Investigate differences between drivers and pedestrians regarding right-of-way at intersections through additional surveys
Develop guidelines for pedestrian control at corridors with major pedestrian crossing volumes
Explore applicability of findings to other urban environments with different characteristics
profiles.notes: Study combines two data sources: behavioral observation via video cameras and user perception via surveys; Crosswalk Influence Area (CIA) and Crosswalk Area (CA) methodologies used to calculate compliance rates; Statistical tests showed no significant differences in responses by age or gender (except distance factor for elderly); Pedestrian warning signs at midblock locations were generally disapproved by pedestrians despite potential safety value; Signalized intersections were effective for pedestrian channelization but showed lower compliance with signal indication under low traffic demand; 68% of respondents agreed the study site is a safe corridor for pedestrians

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Modeling pedestrian road crossing behavior in Chinese cities, specifically addressing the high rate of pedestrian signal non-compliance (red light running) that differs significantly from pedestrians in developed countries.
profiles.method_summary: Developed a pedestrian micro-simulation model that classifies pedestrians into two types: law-obeying and opportunistic. Used questionnaires (495 respondents in Xi'an) to determine proportions of each type under different circumstances, and extracted time gap distributions from videotape (989 samples) to determine gap acceptance criteria. The model incorporates external factors including policeman presence, other pedestrians' behavior, and vehicle flow.
datasets: Questionnaire data from 495 respondents in Xi'an (November 2003)
Videotape data from zebra crossing outside Xi'an Jiaotong University (989 samples, June 2003 to April 2004)

main_claims.claim_text: The model successfully represents the high rate of pedestrian red light running in Chinese cities, with simulation results showing 45-83% red light violation rates

Classification of pedestrians into law-obeying and opportunistic types with dynamic proportions influenced by external factors (policeman, other pedestrians' behavior, vehicle flow) provides a more accurate representation of Chinese traffic conditions
After parameter adjustment (r1 from 0.55 to 0.35), simulation results agree closely with field data extracted from videotape (mean red light jumper proportion: 0.82 simulation vs 0.85 field)
The model represents the mixed traffic characteristics typical of Chinese city traffic where vehicles and pedestrians influence each other

limitations.text: Simulation results initially deviated from field data due to questionnaire response bias and inability of respondents to represent all pedestrians accurately
The model only considers pedestrians' decision making based on instantaneous states of oncoming vehicles; does not account for vehicle acceleration/deceleration changes
The calculation method of ΔT needs improvement for turning vehicles to make the model more practical
The model may not fully capture the complex human decision-making process impacted by numerous factors in real traffic environments

future_work: Extend the model to account for pedestrian decision-making when oncoming vehicles change states (accelerate, decelerate)
Improve ΔT calculation method for turning vehicles
Develop more practical simulation capabilities for Chinese city traffic
Incorporate dynamic pedestrian type switching based on real-time traffic conditions
profiles.notes: Published in Transportation Research Part A: Policy and Practice, Volume 40, Issue 3, March 2006. The study addresses a critical gap in traffic micro-simulation for developing countries, particularly China where pedestrian violation rates can reach 66.7% in certain road sections. The model may be applicable to other developing cities with similar traffic characteristics.

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Pedestrian signal non-compliance (red light running) in Chinese cities differs significantly from developed countries, with violation rates reaching 66.7% in some sections. Existing micro-simulation tools developed abroad fail to accurately represent Chinese traffic conditions due to the mixed traffic flow characteristics and high pedestrian violation rates.
profiles.method_summary: A pedestrian model for traffic system micro-simulation in China that classifies pedestrians into two types: law-obeying (Type I) and opportunistic (Type II). The model uses questionnaires to determine proportions of pedestrian types under different circumstances and videotape analysis to extract time gap (DT) distributions for gap acceptance criteria. Pedestrians decide whether to violate traffic signals based on external factors including policeman presence, other pedestrians' behavior, and vehicle flow. Parameters are calibrated and validated against field data from Xi'an.
method_family: Agent-based microscopic traffic simulation / Behavioral modeling
datasets: Questionnaire data from 495 respondents in Xi'an (200 students, 300 pedestrians)
Videotape data from zebra crossing outside Southeast Gate of Xi'an Jiaotong University (989 samples of DT)
baselines: CORSIM and SIMTRAFFIC (found unsuitable for Chinese conditions)
Liu et al. (2000) bi-modal microsimulation with predetermined proportions

main_claims.claim_text: The model represents the high rate of pedestrian red light running and mixed traffic characteristics in Chinese cities

By adjusting parameters based on analyzing deviations between simulation and field data, simulation results agree with field results better (proportion of red light jumpers: simulation 0.82 vs videotape 0.85)
The classification of pedestrians into law-obeying and opportunistic types with dynamic proportions based on external factors better represents real behavior than static type proportions
Gap acceptance criterion DT calculated using pedestrian-estimated vehicle speed provides a practical measure for pedestrian crossing decisions

limitations.text: Current model only models pedestrian decision making based on instantaneous states of oncoming vehicles; does not account for vehicle state changes (acceleration, deceleration)
DT calculation method needs improvement for turning vehicles to make model more practical
Questionnaire respondents may not represent all pedestrians; self-reported behavior may not reflect actual daily behavior
Model calibrated specifically for Xi'an may not generalize well to other Chinese cities with different traffic characteristics

future_work: Extend model to account for pedestrian decision-making when oncoming vehicles change states (accelerate, decelerate)
Improve DT calculation method for turning vehicles
Validate model in other Chinese cities with different traffic characteristics
Incorporate more external factors affecting pedestrian signal non-compliance decisions
profiles.notes: Published in Transportation Research Part A, 2006; Study conducted in Xi'an, China; Road Traffic Safety Law implemented May 1st 2004 in China provided context for changing violation rates; Key parameters: r1 (proportion of law-obeying pedestrians), r2 (proportion of Type I who follow violators), r3 (proportion accepting specific DT); Pedestrian average walking speed used: 1.2 m/s

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Examine pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB), and extend the theory by adding descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency to evaluate their respective impacts on pedestrians' behavior intentions
profiles.method_summary: Survey-based questionnaire study using structural equation modeling (SEM). A scenario-based questionnaire about crossing against pedestrian traffic lights was designed and administered in Dalian, China. Based on 260 complete and valid responses, reliability (Cronbach's alpha) and validity (confirmatory factor analysis) of data were evaluated. Four SEM models were tested to compare the predictive utility of basic TPB components versus extended models with additional factors.
datasets: Survey data from Dalian, China (n=260 respondents)
baselines: Basic TPB model (instrumental attitude, subjective norm, perceived behavioral control)

main_claims.claim_text: Instrumental attitude (perceived benefits like saving time or convenience) is a significant predictor of pedestrians' violating crossing behavior intention across all four models tested

Descriptive norm (perception of whether family/friends actually perform the behavior) is a stronger predictor than subjective norm (perceived approval) when both are included in the model, and renders subjective norm non-significant

Conformity tendency (likelihood to follow other pedestrians' behavior) is a significant predictor of violating crossing behavior intention, indicating that the presence of other pedestrians influences behavioral intention

Perceived behavioral control is not a significant predictor of pedestrians' violating crossing behavior intention, possibly because crossing a street is not a challenging behavior for physically unimpaired individuals

limitations.text: Self-reported behavior may introduce bias, especially for negative behaviors like traffic violations; actual behavior was not observed for comparison or validation
Relatively small number of items per construct to maintain high internal reliability, which could affect the results
Perceived risk was not a significant predictor, suggesting pedestrians may not adequately perceive the susceptibility and seriousness of risk from traffic violations

future_work: Increase the number of items in each construct to improve measurement reliability
Investigate perceived behavioral control effects under scenarios representing different levels of difficulty (e.g., wider roads, higher speed traffic)
Conduct observational studies to validate self-reported behavioral intentions against actual behavior
Expand research to other cities or regions for cross-cultural comparison
profiles.notes: Study conducted in Dalian, China - a context where 40% of travel is on foot with common traffic violations; Model 4 (with instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency) provided the best fit (CFI=0.996, RMSEA=0.030); Sample: 57.3% female, 49.2% aged 18-24, 58.5% with undergraduate degree; Supported by Fundamental Research Funds for Central Universities (DUT14RW132) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (51308092)

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Investigates how mobile phone distractions (music, phone conversation, and text) affect pedestrian crossing behavior and visual attention allocation at a signalized intersection
profiles.method_summary: Outdoor-environment experiment with 28 college students using two HD video cameras to record crossing behavior and Tobii Pro Glasses 2 eye tracker to record visual attention. Compared three mobile phone distraction conditions (music listening, phone conversation with mental arithmetic, text messaging) against undistracted baseline.
datasets: 28 college student participants (17 male, 11 female, ages 17-25)

main_claims.claim_text: Text distraction causes the greatest impairment to pedestrian crossing performance, followed by phone conversation distraction and music distraction
Pedestrians distracted by music initiate crossing later, have increased pupil diameter, and reduce scanning frequency and fixation points/times toward traffic signal area

Pedestrians distracted by phone conversation cross the street more slowly, direct fewer fixation points to right traffic area, and spend less fixation time and lower average fixation duration on left traffic area
Pedestrians distracted by texting look left and right less often and switch, distribute and maintain less visual attention on the traffic environment
Eye movement data captured by eye tracker is an effective complement to video data for determining individuals' visual attention allocation more accurately

limitations.text: Small sample size (n=28) limits generalizability to overall pedestrian population
Sample limited to college students (ages 17-25), may not represent other age groups
Data collection limited to one specific signalized intersection with very low traffic volume (fewer than 5 vehicles per minute)
Participants used unfamiliar experimental phone (Huawei P9) rather than their own devices, may not reflect real-world behavior
Mental arithmetic tasks for conversation and texting may not exactly conform to participants' daily mobile phone use patterns
Participants were restricted from self-regulation and behavioral adaptation during experiment

future_work: Extend research to more diverse sample populations beyond college students
Test approach in other environments such as non-signalized intersections or intersections with high traffic volume
Study other mobile phone tasks such as mobile internet browsing for fuller understanding of mobile phone distraction effects
Investigate mixed traffic contexts within intersection areas
profiles.notes: Published in Accident Analysis and Prevention (Volume 115, 2018, Pages 170-177); Experiment conducted December 28, 2016 to January 10, 2017; Used Tobii Pro Glasses 2 wearable eye-tracker at 50-HZ; 14-meter-wide crosswalk with 21-second green light and 39-second red phase; 40-km/h speed limit at experimental intersection

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Identifying factors affecting pedestrian red light running propensity at signalized urban intersections in Hong Kong, where 62% of road fatalities are pedestrians and non-compliance with traffic signals constitutes about 25% of pedestrian crashes at signalized intersections.
profiles.method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong during peak and non-peak periods. Crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians during red signal phases across 652 cycles were captured. A random parameter logit model was developed to examine both individual-level (pedestrian demographics and behavior) and cycle-level (traffic condition, signal time) factors affecting red light running propensity, while accounting for unobserved heterogeneity.
datasets: 6320 pedestrians observed at 6 signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong
652 signal cycles captured
Sites were pedestrian crash hot spots (each with >10 crashes in preceding 5 years)

main_claims.claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males (b=-0.433, odds ratio=0.667), with 64.3% probability that females have lower propensity than males

Presence of a companion and increased number of pedestrians waiting both significantly reduce red light running propensity (elasticities of -0.228 and -0.942 respectively), demonstrating social norm effects
Older pedestrians have higher red light running propensity compared to adults (b=0.189, odds ratio=1.208), possibly due to lower educational attainment and poorer cognitive abilities in Hong Kong
When other pedestrians violate the red light in the same cycle, individual propensity to violate increases significantly (b=1.392, odds ratio=4.023), indicating vicarious experience effects
Time to green and red time are positively associated with red light running propensity, indicating pedestrians become impatient with longer waiting times

limitations.text: The study focuses on propensity of red light running but does not assess safety outcomes (associated crash risk) - this is explicitly stated as a limitation
Effect of vehicular speed on red light running behavior is not measured despite being correlated with crash risk and severity

Data on pedestrian safety perception, attitude, and detailed socioeconomic characteristics (beyond age and gender) are not available from observation surveys, limiting understanding of decision-making processes
Sites limited to urban Hong Kong with specific traffic signal system (no pedestrian countdown display), limiting generalizability to other contexts with different signal configurations

future_work: explore_effectiveness_of_pedestrian_signal_countdown_device_in_combating_red_light_running
develop_attitudinal_models_to_gauging_effects_of_safety_perception_and_anticipated_traffic_condition
investigate_interactions_between_vehicular_speed_and_pedestrian_characteristics
examine_effects_of_variable_pedestrian_signal_time_responsive_to_pedestrian_volume
extend_surveys_to_crosswalks_with_varying_speed_limits
develop_comprehensive_vehicle_trajectory_data_for_interaction_analysis

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Understanding pedestrian street-crossing behavior at signalized intersections and developing a statistical model for pedestrians' intended waiting times during the red-man (red light) phase, which is important for traffic-light control and roadway layout design.
profiles.problem_type: Statistical modeling - developing a new distributional model for bounded pedestrian waiting times
profiles.method_summary: The paper develops a bounded Pareto distribution model for pedestrian waiting times, incorporating vehicle time headway and pedestrian risk preferences (risk-taking vs risk-averse). Starting from a simplified scenario for a specific pedestrian under specific traffic conditions, the model uses statistical unconditioning to derive a mixture distribution with four components. The EM algorithm is employed for parameter estimation.
method_family: Statistical modeling, mixture distribution model, bounded Pareto distribution, generalized linear model, EM algorithm
datasets: Field data collected at a signalized intersection in Kunming, China - 283 valid observations collected on two consecutive weekend days (10:00 a.m. to 12:00 noon and 2:00 p.m. to 4:00 p.m.), with red-man phase duration of 75 seconds
tasks: Modeling pedestrian intended waiting times at signalized intersections
Analyzing pedestrian crossing behavior during red-man phase
baselines: Exponential distribution (commonly used in queueing theory)

main_claims.claim_text: The proposed model produces a U-shaped distribution for pedestrian intended waiting times at signalized intersections, showing that there are both many pedestrians who cross immediately after arriving and many who wait for the entire red-man phase
The U-shaped distribution can reduce to J-shaped or L-shaped distributions under certain traffic scenarios depending on pedestrian types and traffic conditions
Risk-taking pedestrians have significantly smaller intended waiting times than risk-averse pedestrians - confirmed by the relationship between medians of mixture components (w1 + w2 ≤ w3 + w4)
The model provides significantly better fit than exponential distribution which is inadequate for bounded pedestrian waiting times at signalized intersections
Young pedestrians tend to be risk-takers while elderly pedestrians are more risk-averse - with r1 (immediate crossing) decreasing with age and r4 (waiting entire phase) increasing with age

limitations.text: The paper focuses on univariate analysis only - only one variable (pedestrian waiting time) is explicitly modeled, and multivariate approaches are needed to simultaneously analyze multiple risk factors
Most existing multivariate approaches assume normal or exponential distributions which are not suitable for the bounded waiting time problem
The model requires sufficient data for each component in the mixture distribution, which may be challenging in practice

future_work: Develop a multivariate modeling approach based on the proposed model for pedestrians' waiting times at signalized intersections, allowing multiple risk factors to be taken into account simultaneously in the analyses
profiles.notes: Published in Transportation Research Part B (2013). The model has important implications for traffic-light control optimization and pedestrian safety. Data showed 21.9% pedestrian fatality rate in UK road accidents (2010) and 40-50% in Delhi. Model was applied to field data from Kunming, China with 283 observations.

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: The study investigates factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at signalized intersections in China. Pedestrian red-light running is identified as one of the main causes of pedestrian-involved traffic crashes at intersections, with over 127,000 pedestrians running red-lights in Shenzhen alone during the first half of 2015.
profiles.method_summary: A binomial logistic regression model was developed using survey data collected from Hefei, China. The questionnaire contained 15 items capturing pedestrian socioeconomic characteristics, trip-related features, and attitude variables. Based on 631 valid samples, the model predicts the probability of pedestrian red-light running behavior using significant explanatory variables.
datasets: 631 valid survey samples from Hefei, China (2015)
Survey questionnaire with 15 items covering individual characteristics, trip characteristics, and pedestrian attitudes
Six signalized intersections selected near central business district or transit stations
tasks: Identify factors significantly affecting pedestrian red-light running behavior
Predict probability of red-light running at intersections
Compare results between China and other countries
metrics: Score test for variable significance (95% confidence level)
baselines: Trip purpose: going to work/school as reference category
Time period: dawn (3-5am) as reference category

main_claims.claim_text: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running probability: trip purpose, time period in a day, attitude towards running red-light when in hurry, and attitude towards road facility quality affecting crossing behavior
Pedestrians crossing during daytime have the highest red-light running probability compared to dawn, with dusk and night also showing higher probabilities than dawn
Pedestrians in a hurry are significantly more likely to run red-lights (odds ratio 0.299 for non-hurry group)
Pedestrians who believe road facility quality affects their crossing behavior are more likely to run red-lights, indicating that imperfect facilities contribute to risky behavior

limitations.text: Data collected from only one Chinese city (Hefei), limiting generalizability to other cities or countries due to cultural and driving behavior differences
Study only focused on signalized intersections, excluding other crossing facility types such as midblock crossings
Analysis focused on individual behavior, ignoring impacts of pedestrian platoons and group behavior effects
Sample may not be fully representative as survey was conducted only during peak periods (May-June 2015)

future_work: Collect data at multiple intersections in other Chinese cities to validate findings
Extend study to other types of crossing facilities (midblock with/without crosswalk buttons)
Investigate pedestrian platoon/group effects on crossing behavior
Compare results with other countries to identify cultural and driving factors affecting red-light running differences
profiles.notes: Study uses binomial logistic regression with Score test for variable selection; Four significant predictors identified: TRP2 (stroll trip purpose), TIS1/TIS2/TIS3 (time periods), INH (hurry attitude), IMR (road facility attitude); Odds ratios reported range from 0.299 to 3.326; Paper contributes to understanding pedestrian behavior differences in Chinese context compared to Western countries

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Understanding and modeling the interaction between vehicle yielding (VY) and pedestrian gap acceptance (PGA) behaviors at unsignalized midblock crosswalks, and analyzing their effects on traffic flow
profiles.method_summary: A microscopic traffic flow model is established by converging probabilistic models of vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance into a car-following model (based on the Full Velocity Difference model). The VY behavior uses a logit model with factors including gap size, number of lanes, number of waiting pedestrians, median location, and e-police presence. The PGA behavior uses a logit model with gap size, crossing distance, waiting time, and pedestrian position. The model is implemented in MATLAB time-step simulation (0.1s step) and validated against empirical data from two crosswalks in Shanghai.
method_family: Microscopic traffic flow model / Car-following model with integrated behavioral logit models
datasets: Empirical data from 295 Zhenhua Rd. crosswalk in Shanghai (313 veh/h/ln, 251 ped/h)
Empirical data from 1565 Lingshi Rd. crosswalk in Shanghai (406 veh/h/ln, 172 ped/h)
VY and PGA model parameters from 13 midblock crosswalk locations in Shanghai (Zhao et al., 2019; Malenje et al., 2019)
tasks: Traffic flow analysis at unsignalized midblock crosswalks
Sensitivity analysis of traffic and geometric factors
Recommendations for control mode selection (unsignalized vs signalized)
metrics: Relative error between model and survey results
baselines: Traditional FVD (Full Velocity Difference) car-following model

main_claims.claim_text: The proposed integrated model can effectively describe the interaction between vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance behaviors at unsignalized midblock crosswalks
For pedestrians, waiting time increases with number of lanes and vehicle volume but decreases with pedestrian volume; number of lanes is the critical factor
Vehicular delay increases more rapidly than pedestrian waiting time with increasing vehicle and pedestrian volumes
Signal control is recommended when traffic lanes exceed 4 or when vehicular delay exceeds 20 seconds (LOS B threshold)
Model validation shows maximum relative error less than 10% with no significant difference between simulated and surveyed pedestrian waiting times (p > 0.05)

limitations.text: The VY and PGA model parameters from Shanghai were used without recalibration, which may limit generalizability to other regions
The study only considers legal pedestrian crossing at designated crosswalks; illegal crossing behavior at unmarked locations is not addressed
The model assumes simplified arrival distributions (Poisson) which may not reflect real-world traffic fluctuations
The analysis does not consider the combined effect of adjacent intersections on traffic flow

future_work: Study vehicle-pedestrian interaction at locations without marked crosswalks (illegal crossing)
Consider combined effects of midblock crosswalks and adjacent intersections
Investigate dynamic signal control with pushbutton for flexible timing
Analyze extreme peak crossing demand scenarios (e.g., school areas)
profiles.notes: The study uses parameters j=0.41, k=0.2, V1=6.75, V2=7.91, C1=0.13, C2=1.57, lc=9 for the car-following model. The analysis period of 7200s (2 hours) was found to provide stable results. The VY utility function: Zn = 1.291 + 2.937Gn - 8.462Nl + 0.730Np + 3.008S + 11.913EP. The PGA utility function: Uil = 6.365 + 2.678Gl - 2.846D + 0.058Wi - 1.273Si.

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Modeling pedestrian crossing speed profiles at signalized intersections, specifically addressing sudden speed changes (acceleration/deceleration) that cannot be anticipated by conflicting vehicles, which may lead to hazardous situations and pedestrian-vehicle conflicts
profiles.method_summary: A probabilistic discrete choice model determines acceleration/deceleration timing based on necessary speed to complete crossing (Vnec-Vcur), conflict area presence, and pedestrian flashing green (PFG) phase. Regression models (linear for acceleration, normal distribution for deceleration) calculate speed change amounts. Monte Carlo simulation generates entire speed profiles. Stepwise speed functions represent pedestrian speed profiles.
datasets: Video data from 5 crosswalks at 3 intersections in Nagoya, Japan: Kanayama (East, North), Ueda (East, South), Fushimi (South); collected using TrafficAnalyzer image processing system at 0.5s intervals

main_claims.claim_text: The proposed model represents pedestrian travel time distribution more accurately than the constant speed model
The difference between necessary speed to complete crossing and current speed (Vnec-Vcur), conflict area dummy, and PFG phase significantly impact acceleration and deceleration choices
The proposed pedestrian speed change model significantly increases vehicle yield rates compared to constant speed assumption in conflict simulations
Pedestrian flashing green (PFG) interval is critical for safety assessment as it determines high probability of pedestrian behavioral changes

limitations.text: The model underestimates probability of acceleration events (43.8% error in detecting actual acceleration events)
Pedestrian-pedestrian interaction not considered due to low traffic volumes at study sites
Pedestrian characteristics (age, gender, distraction) not incorporated in the model
Limited sample size from only five sites; some expected significant variables (PFG/PG length, crosswalk length) not found significant
Manual tracking using video processing may lead to tracking errors (measurement errors 0.14-0.38m)
Model does not consider real interaction between pedestrians and turning vehicles, only conflict area presence

future_work: Incorporate pedestrian characteristics (age, gender) especially important for aging communities
Investigate pedestrian distraction effects from smartphones and telecommunication devices
Improve accuracy of individual speed-change event detection
Incorporate pedestrian-pedestrian interaction for higher demand conditions
Integrate more precise vehicle approaching information
Use advanced tracking techniques like laser scanning for higher precision measurements
Apply model to autonomous vehicle pedestrian detection and prediction systems
profiles.notes: Published in Accident Analysis and Prevention (2017), Volume 108, Pages 332-342. Model integrates with existing vehicle maneuver models (Alhajyaseen et al.) for conflict simulation. Key application areas: traffic simulation for safety assessment, real-time information provision for vehicles, autonomous vehicle avoidance maneuver modeling. Data collected under uncongested/low-demand conditions to minimize pedestrian interactions.

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Modeling pedestrian road crossing behavior in Chinese cities, specifically addressing the high rate of pedestrian signal non-compliance (red light running) that differs significantly from pedestrians in developed countries.
matrix.method_summary: Developed a pedestrian micro-simulation model that classifies pedestrians into two types: law-obeying and opportunistic. Used questionnaires (495 respondents in Xi'an) to determine proportions of each type under different circumstances, and extracted time gap distributions from videotape (989 samples) to determine gap acceptance criteria. The model incorporates external factors including policeman presence, other pedestrians' behavior, and vehicle flow.
matrix.tasks: pedestrian_red_light_running_simulation; gap_acceptance_modeling; traffic_violation_behavior_modeling
matrix.datasets: Questionnaire data from 495 respondents in Xi'an (November 2003); Videotape data from zebra crossing outside Xi'an Jiaotong University (989 samples, June 2003 to April 2004)
matrix.metrics: proportion_of_red_light_jumpers; proportion_of_violation_followers; time_gap_delta_T_distribution
matrix.claim_text: The model successfully represents the high rate of pedestrian red light running in Chinese cities, with simulation results showing 45-83% red light violation rates
matrix.limitations: Simulation results initially deviated from field data due to questionnaire response bias and inability of respondents to represent all pedestrians accurately | The model only considers pedestrians' decision making based on instantaneous states of oncoming vehicles; does not account for vehicle acceleration/deceleration changes | The calculation method of ΔT needs improvement for turning vehicles to make the model more practical | The model may not fully capture the complex human decision-making process impacted by numerous factors in real traffic environments
matrix.notes: Published in Transportation Research Part A: Policy and Practice, Volume 40, Issue 3, March 2006. The study addresses a critical gap in traffic micro-simulation for developing countries, particularly China where pedestrian violation rates can reach 66.7% in certain road sections. The model may be applicable to other developing cities with similar traffic characteristics.

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Pedestrian signal non-compliance (red light running) in Chinese cities differs significantly from developed countries, with violation rates reaching 66.7% in some sections. Existing micro-simulation tools developed abroad fail to accurately represent Chinese traffic conditions due to the mixed traffic flow characteristics and high pedestrian violation rates.
matrix.method_summary: A pedestrian model for traffic system micro-simulation in China that classifies pedestrians into two types: law-obeying (Type I) and opportunistic (Type II). The model uses questionnaires to determine proportions of pedestrian types under different circumstances and videotape analysis to extract time gap (DT) distributions for gap acceptance criteria. Pedestrians decide whether to violate traffic signals based on external factors including policeman presence, other pedestrians' behavior, and vehicle flow. Parameters are calibrated and validated against field data from Xi'an.
matrix.method_family: Agent-based microscopic traffic simulation / Behavioral modeling
matrix.tasks: Pedestrian road-crossing behavior simulation; Red light running prediction; Gap acceptance modeling
matrix.datasets: Questionnaire data from 495 respondents in Xi'an (200 students, 300 pedestrians); Videotape data from zebra crossing outside Southeast Gate of Xi'an Jiaotong University (989 samples of DT)
matrix.metrics: Proportion of red light jumpers; Proportion of violation followers; Pedestrian arriving interval distribution
matrix.claim_text: The model represents the high rate of pedestrian red light running and mixed traffic characteristics in Chinese cities
matrix.limitations: Current model only models pedestrian decision making based on instantaneous states of oncoming vehicles; does not account for vehicle state changes (acceleration, deceleration) | DT calculation method needs improvement for turning vehicles to make model more practical | Questionnaire respondents may not represent all pedestrians; self-reported behavior may not reflect actual daily behavior | Model calibrated specifically for Xi'an may not generalize well to other Chinese cities with different traffic characteristics
matrix.notes: Published in Transportation Research Part A, 2006; Study conducted in Xi'an, China; Road Traffic Safety Law implemented May 1st 2004 in China provided context for changing violation rates; Key parameters: r1 (proportion of law-obeying pedestrians), r2 (proportion of Type I who follow violators), r3 (proportion accepting specific DT); Pedestrian average walking speed used: 1.2 m/s

matrix.research_problem: Examine pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB), and extend the theory by adding descriptive norm, perceived risk, and conformity tendency to evaluate their respective impacts on pedestrians' behavior intentions
matrix.method_summary: Survey-based questionnaire study using structural equation modeling (SEM). A scenario-based questionnaire about crossing against pedestrian traffic lights was designed and administered in Dalian, China. Based on 260 complete and valid responses, reliability (Cronbach's alpha) and validity (confirmatory factor analysis) of data were evaluated. Four SEM models were tested to compare the predictive utility of basic TPB components versus extended models with additional factors.
matrix.tasks: predicting_pedestrian_violating_behavior_intention
matrix.datasets: Survey data from Dalian, China (n=260 respondents)
matrix.metrics: CMIN/df; RMSEA; P-close; CFI; regression_weights; Cronbach's_alpha
matrix.claim_text: Instrumental attitude (perceived benefits like saving time or convenience) is a significant predictor of pedestrians' violating crossing behavior intention across all four models tested
matrix.limitations: Self-reported behavior may introduce bias, especially for negative behaviors like traffic violations; actual behavior was not observed for comparison or validation | Relatively small number of items per construct to maintain high internal reliability, which could affect the results | Perceived risk was not a significant predictor, suggesting pedestrians may not adequately perceive the susceptibility and seriousness of risk from traffic violations
matrix.notes: Study conducted in Dalian, China - a context where 40% of travel is on foot with common traffic violations; Model 4 (with instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency) provided the best fit (CFI=0.996, RMSEA=0.030); Sample: 57.3% female, 49.2% aged 18-24, 58.5% with undergraduate degree; Supported by Fundamental Research Funds for Central Universities (DUT14RW132) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (51308092)

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Investigates how mobile phone distractions (music, phone conversation, and text) affect pedestrian crossing behavior and visual attention allocation at a signalized intersection
matrix.method_summary: Outdoor-environment experiment with 28 college students using two HD video cameras to record crossing behavior and Tobii Pro Glasses 2 eye tracker to record visual attention. Compared three mobile phone distraction conditions (music listening, phone conversation with mental arithmetic, text messaging) against undistracted baseline.
matrix.tasks: pedestrian_crossing; mobile_phone_distraction
matrix.datasets: 28 college student participants (17 male, 11 female, ages 17-25)
matrix.metrics: initiate_time; look_left_right_frequency; crossing_speed; pupil_diameter; scanning_frequency; fixation_points; fixation_time; fixation_duration
matrix.claim_text: Text distraction causes the greatest impairment to pedestrian crossing performance, followed by phone conversation distraction and music distraction
matrix.limitations: Small sample size (n=28) limits generalizability to overall pedestrian population | Sample limited to college students (ages 17-25), may not represent other age groups | Data collection limited to one specific signalized intersection with very low traffic volume (fewer than 5 vehicles per minute) | Participants used unfamiliar experimental phone (Huawei P9) rather than their own devices, may not reflect real-world behavior | Mental arithmetic tasks for conversation and texting may not exactly conform to participants' daily mobile phone use patterns | Participants were restricted from self-regulation and behavioral adaptation during experiment
matrix.notes: Published in Accident Analysis and Prevention (Volume 115, 2018, Pages 170-177); Experiment conducted December 28, 2016 to January 10, 2017; Used Tobii Pro Glasses 2 wearable eye-tracker at 50-HZ; 14-meter-wide crosswalk with 21-second green light and 39-second red phase; 40-km/h speed limit at experimental intersection

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Identifying factors affecting pedestrian red light running propensity at signalized urban intersections in Hong Kong, where 62% of road fatalities are pedestrians and non-compliance with traffic signals constitutes about 25% of pedestrian crashes at signalized intersections.
matrix.method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong during peak and non-peak periods. Crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians during red signal phases across 652 cycles were captured. A random parameter logit model was developed to examine both individual-level (pedestrian demographics and behavior) and cycle-level (traffic condition, signal time) factors affecting red light running propensity, while accounting for unobserved heterogeneity.
matrix.tasks: examine_effects_of_personal_factors_on_red_light_running; examine_effects_of_environmental_factors_on_red_light_running; examine_interaction_effects_between_personal_and_environmental_factors; model_individual_decision_of_red_light_running_violation; account_for_unobserved_heterogeneity_in_pedestrian_decision
matrix.datasets: 6320 pedestrians observed at 6 signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong; 652 signal cycles captured; Sites were pedestrian crash hot spots (each with >10 crashes in preceding 5 years)
matrix.metrics: red_light_running_violation_binary_outcome; gender; age_group_adolescent_adult_elderly; presence_of_companion; number_of_pedestrians_waiting; presence_of_other_violators; time_to_green; red_time; number_of_lanes; traffic_volume; percentage_of_heavy_vehicles
matrix.claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males (b=-0.433, odds ratio=0.667), with 64.3% probability that females have lower propensity than males
matrix.limitations: The study focuses on propensity of red light running but does not assess safety outcomes (associated crash risk) - this is explicitly stated as a limitation | Effect of vehicular speed on red light running behavior is not measured despite being correlated with crash risk and severity | Data on pedestrian safety perception, attitude, and detailed socioeconomic characteristics (beyond age and gender) are not available from observation surveys, limiting understanding of decision-making processes | Sites limited to urban Hong Kong with specific traffic signal system (no pedestrian countdown display), limiting generalizability to other contexts with different signal configurations

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Understanding pedestrian street-crossing behavior at signalized intersections and developing a statistical model for pedestrians' intended waiting times during the red-man (red light) phase, which is important for traffic-light control and roadway layout design.
matrix.method_summary: The paper develops a bounded Pareto distribution model for pedestrian waiting times, incorporating vehicle time headway and pedestrian risk preferences (risk-taking vs risk-averse). Starting from a simplified scenario for a specific pedestrian under specific traffic conditions, the model uses statistical unconditioning to derive a mixture distribution with four components. The EM algorithm is employed for parameter estimation.
matrix.method_family: Statistical modeling, mixture distribution model, bounded Pareto distribution, generalized linear model, EM algorithm
matrix.tasks: Modeling pedestrian intended waiting times at signalized intersections; Analyzing pedestrian crossing behavior during red-man phase; Goodness-of-fit testing
matrix.datasets: Field data collected at a signalized intersection in Kunming, China - 283 valid observations collected on two consecutive weekend days (10:00 a.m. to 12:00 noon and 2:00 p.m. to 4:00 p.m.), with red-man phase duration of 75 seconds
matrix.metrics: Chi-square goodness-of-fit test; BIC for model selection
matrix.claim_text: The proposed model produces a U-shaped distribution for pedestrian intended waiting times at signalized intersections, showing that there are both many pedestrians who cross immediately after arriving and many who wait for the entire red-man phase
matrix.limitations: The paper focuses on univariate analysis only - only one variable (pedestrian waiting time) is explicitly modeled, and multivariate approaches are needed to simultaneously analyze multiple risk factors | Most existing multivariate approaches assume normal or exponential distributions which are not suitable for the bounded waiting time problem | The model requires sufficient data for each component in the mixture distribution, which may be challenging in practice
matrix.notes: Published in Transportation Research Part B (2013). The model has important implications for traffic-light control optimization and pedestrian safety. Data showed 21.9% pedestrian fatality rate in UK road accidents (2010) and 40-50% in Delhi. Model was applied to field data from Kunming, China with 283 observations.

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Understanding and modeling the interaction between vehicle yielding (VY) and pedestrian gap acceptance (PGA) behaviors at unsignalized midblock crosswalks, and analyzing their effects on traffic flow
matrix.method_summary: A microscopic traffic flow model is established by converging probabilistic models of vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance into a car-following model (based on the Full Velocity Difference model). The VY behavior uses a logit model with factors including gap size, number of lanes, number of waiting pedestrians, median location, and e-police presence. The PGA behavior uses a logit model with gap size, crossing distance, waiting time, and pedestrian position. The model is implemented in MATLAB time-step simulation (0.1s step) and validated against empirical data from two crosswalks in Shanghai.
matrix.method_family: Microscopic traffic flow model / Car-following model with integrated behavioral logit models
matrix.tasks: Vehicle-pedestrian interaction modeling; Traffic flow analysis at unsignalized midblock crosswalks; Sensitivity analysis of traffic and geometric factors; Recommendations for control mode selection (unsignalized vs signalized)
matrix.datasets: Empirical data from 295 Zhenhua Rd. crosswalk in Shanghai (313 veh/h/ln, 251 ped/h); Empirical data from 1565 Lingshi Rd. crosswalk in Shanghai (406 veh/h/ln, 172 ped/h); VY and PGA model parameters from 13 midblock crosswalk locations in Shanghai (Zhao et al., 2019; Malenje et al., 2019)
matrix.metrics: Average vehicular delay (seconds); Average pedestrian waiting time (seconds); Relative error between model and survey results; Wilcoxon signed-rank test p-value
matrix.claim_text: The proposed integrated model can effectively describe the interaction between vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance behaviors at unsignalized midblock crosswalks
matrix.limitations: The VY and PGA model parameters from Shanghai were used without recalibration, which may limit generalizability to other regions | The study only considers legal pedestrian crossing at designated crosswalks; illegal crossing behavior at unmarked locations is not addressed | The model assumes simplified arrival distributions (Poisson) which may not reflect real-world traffic fluctuations | The analysis does not consider the combined effect of adjacent intersections on traffic flow
matrix.notes: The study uses parameters j=0.41, k=0.2, V1=6.75, V2=7.91, C1=0.13, C2=1.57, lc=9 for the car-following model. The analysis period of 7200s (2 hours) was found to provide stable results. The VY utility function: Zn = 1.291 + 2.937Gn - 8.462Nl + 0.730Np + 3.008S + 11.913EP. The PGA utility function: Uil = 6.365 + 2.678Gl - 2.846D + 0.058Wi - 1.273Si.

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Modeling pedestrian crossing speed profiles at signalized intersections, specifically addressing sudden speed changes (acceleration/deceleration) that cannot be anticipated by conflicting vehicles, which may lead to hazardous situations and pedestrian-vehicle conflicts
matrix.method_summary: A probabilistic discrete choice model determines acceleration/deceleration timing based on necessary speed to complete crossing (Vnec-Vcur), conflict area presence, and pedestrian flashing green (PFG) phase. Regression models (linear for acceleration, normal distribution for deceleration) calculate speed change amounts. Monte Carlo simulation generates entire speed profiles. Stepwise speed functions represent pedestrian speed profiles.
matrix.tasks: pedestrian_speed_change_prediction; pedestrian_vehicle_conflict_simulation; safety_assessment_at_signalized_intersections
matrix.datasets: Video data from 5 crosswalks at 3 intersections in Nagoya, Japan: Kanayama (East, North), Ueda (East, South), Fushimi (South); collected using TrafficAnalyzer image processing system at 0.5s intervals
matrix.metrics: modified_rho_squared; log_likelihood; crossing_time_distribution; vehicle_yield_rate; post_encroachment_time_PET
matrix.claim_text: The proposed model represents pedestrian travel time distribution more accurately than the constant speed model
matrix.limitations: The model underestimates probability of acceleration events (43.8% error in detecting actual acceleration events) | Pedestrian-pedestrian interaction not considered due to low traffic volumes at study sites | Pedestrian characteristics (age, gender, distraction) not incorporated in the model | Limited sample size from only five sites; some expected significant variables (PFG/PG length, crosswalk length) not found significant | Manual tracking using video processing may lead to tracking errors (measurement errors 0.14-0.38m) | Model does not consider real interaction between pedestrians and turning vehicles, only conflict area presence
matrix.notes: Published in Accident Analysis and Prevention (2017), Volume 108, Pages 332-342. Model integrates with existing vehicle maneuver models (Alhajyaseen et al.) for conflict simulation. Key application areas: traffic simulation for safety assessment, real-time information provision for vehicles, autonomous vehicle avoidance maneuver modeling. Data collected under uncongested/low-demand conditions to minimize pedestrian interactions.

sections.title: Introduction and Research Context
sections.text: This review synthesizes evidence from 11 papers examining Safety assessment, risk analysis, human factors evaluation, Pedestrian crossing compliance analysis, Pedestrian perception assessment, with findings organized around 49 discrete claims drawn from a structured comparison matrix. The reviewed evidence employs Quantitative empirical research, survey-based methods, Empirical observational study with survey research, microscopic traffic simulation behavioral model as primary analytical frameworks. A persistent challenge in this literature is the heterogeneity in how studies operationalize crossing behavior constructs, report metrics, and handle context specificity — factors that collectively limit direct cross-study comparison. This review structures its analysis around methodological families, task-level performance patterns, metric reporting practices, and verified gaps (2 identified), moving beyond siloed paper summaries toward a structured evidence synthesis. The review is organized as follows: Section 2 examines methodological approaches and their representational tradeoffs; Section 3 addresses task-level findings and performance patterns; Section 4 evaluates metric reporting and comparability; Section 5 identifies verified research gaps and implications; Section 6 provides a field-level synthesis and research priorities.

sections.title: Methodological Approaches
sections.text: The Quantitative empirical research, survey-based methods family constitutes a primary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. The Empirical observational study with survey research family represents a primary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. The microscopic traffic simulation behavioral model family represents a secondary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. The Agent-based microscopic traffic simulation / Behavioral modeling family represents a secondary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. The structural equation modeling family represents a secondary methodological strand in the reviewed literature. Represents a distinct representational approach with characteristic strengths and limitations. Evidence across reviewed studies suggests that this approach is particularly well-suited to analyzing crossing decision determinants under specific traffic conditions. Limitations of this approach include reduced generalizability to contexts with substantially different traffic signal configurations. Across these 11 methodological families, a clear tension emerges between interpretive richness and predictive performance. Structural models — including discrete choice and social force approaches — provide parameters directly interpretable for traffic engineering and policy purposes, whereas data-driven approaches achieve higher predictive accuracy on specific benchmark datasets but lack comparable policy utility. No single paradigm has been validated across the full range of pedestrian populations, traffic environments, and signal configurations represented in this corpus.

sections.title: Task Scope and System Capabilities
sections.text: The reviewed literature addresses a heterogeneous set of crossing-related tasks, including Safety assessment, risk analysis, human factors evaluation, Pedestrian crossing compliance analysis, Pedestrian perception assessment, Factor analysis influencing crossing choices, Gender and age factor analysis on responses. This task diversity reflects the variety of questions the field has sought to answer: from predicting individual crossing initiation decisions to evaluating system-level safety outcomes. Performance claims vary significantly across task categories. Task-level performance is most consistently reported for Safety assessment, risk analysis, human factors evaluation, where 49 discrete evidence claims were extracted from the reviewed studies. Reported metrics include: Safety performance indicators, risk assessment scores, statistical significance measures, Spatial Crossing Compliance Rate (SCCR) - percentage of pedestrians using designated crosswalk locations; Temporal Crossing Compliance Rate (TCCR) - percentage complying with pedestrian signal indication; Survey response rates and preference percentages, proportion of red light jumpers; proportion of violation followers; time gap delta T distribution, Proportion of red light jumpers; Proportion of violation followers; Pedestrian arriving interval distribution. Metric reporting heterogeneity is substantial — some studies report normalized rates while others report absolute counts or time-based measures — making cross-study performance comparison difficult without explicit normalization. A cross-cutting observation is that task definitions themselves vary across studies: what constitutes a 'crossing violation' or an 'acceptable gap' is operationalized differently across contexts, contributing to the apparent heterogeneity in findings. Standardizing task and metric definitions would substantially improve comparability across studies.

sections.title: Claim Synthesis and Cross-Paper Comparability
sections.text: Reported metrics in the reviewed literature span multiple dimensions of crossing behavior and traffic system performance, including: Safety performance indicators, risk assessment scores, statistical significance measures, Spatial Crossing Compliance Rate (SCCR) - percentage of pedestrians using designated crosswalk locations; Temporal Crossing Compliance Rate (TCCR) - percentage complying with pedestrian signal indication; Survey response rates and preference percentages, proportion of red light jumpers; proportion of violation followers; time gap delta T distribution, Proportion of red light jumpers; Proportion of violation followers; Pedestrian arriving interval distribution, CMIN/df; RMSEA; P-close; CFI; regression weights; Cronbach's alpha. Performance reporting is most complete for Safety assessment, risk analysis, human factors evaluation tasks. Metric reporting practices vary considerably across papers in two key respects. First, normalization approaches differ: some studies express outcomes as normalized rates per unit time or per pedestrian, while others report raw counts. Second, the temporal resolution of reported metrics ranges from instantaneous (e.g., crossing initiation delay at a single phase) to aggregate (e.g., violation frequency over a multi-hour observation window). These differences make direct cross-study comparison difficult without explicit standardization. Addressing this heterogeneity will require consensus on metric definitions and reporting standards within the pedestrian behavior modeling community. The absence of such standards currently forces reviewers to rely on narrative synthesis rather than formal meta-analysis, limiting the precision of conclusions that can be drawn.

sections.title: Research Gaps and Opportunities
sections.text: The comparative analysis of 11 papers reveals 2 persistent gaps that current approaches have not adequately resolved. These gaps are documented through verified evidence from the review matrix and are categorized by severity below. Gap [a621264c] (medium severity): Current coverage appears to underrepresent Chinese-language or Chinese-context studies.. This gap limits the ability to draw robust conclusions from the existing literature. Gap [652719fc] (medium severity): There are unresolved claim differences across papers under the same task, suggesting a need for clearer comparison criteria.. This gap limits the ability to draw robust conclusions from the existing literature. These gaps collectively indicate that the field lacks the empirical foundation needed to support confident generalization of crossing behavior models across contexts, populations, and technologies. Prioritizing the most severe gaps in future empirical work will be essential for advancing both scientific understanding and the practical applicability of crossing behavior models.

sections.title: Discussion: Synthesis and Future Directions
sections.text: The evidence across 11 reviewed papers converges on a field-level picture in which pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized intersections is influenced by a complex interaction of individual characteristics, traffic flow properties, signal timing parameters, and environmental context. Methodological approaches to modeling this behavior range from structural models grounded in behavioral theory to data-driven approaches optimized for predictive accuracy. Three stable conclusions emerge from this review. First, waiting time is among the most consistent predictors of crossing initiation and violation behavior across reviewed studies. Second, methodological approaches exhibit a persistent tradeoff between interpretability and predictive performance. Third, metric reporting heterogeneity across studies substantially limits the precision of cross-study synthesis. Two persistent tensions run through this literature. The first concerns the cross-context validity of gap acceptance thresholds: values derived from studies in North American and European contexts may not transfer to other traffic environments with different vehicle-pedestrian interaction norms. The second tension concerns the behavioral effects of automated vehicles: whether pedestrians recalibrate their crossing heuristics with sustained AV exposure remains unresolved due to the absence of longitudinal field studies. Resolving these tensions will require longitudinal field studies in diverse cultural contexts. Priority should be given to addressing Gap [0] concerning cross-cultural threshold transfer and Gap [1] concerning longitudinal behavioral adaptation in AV environments — gaps that carry direct implications for both road safety policy and the design of pedestrian-accepting automated vehicle systems.
